subsystem: A privileged disk/partition helper method, Helper::getPartitionSizeInfo(), that queries the used/free/block-size of a partition by shelling out to external tools. For mounted partitions it runs the coreutils 'df' command; for unmounted partitions it runs 'partclone' (via getPartcloneExecuter/DDevicePartInfo) and scrapes its stderr. It parses the tool output back into caller-supplied out-parameters (used, free, blockSize).
provenance: Identified by the class/method name Helper::getPartitionSizeInfo, the helper types DDevicePartInfo and getPartcloneExecuter(), the dCError() logging macro, Qt types (QString, QProcess, QByteArray, QRegularExpression), and the use of the 'partclone' disk-imaging tool writing '/tmp/partclone.log'. This is the deepin desktop stack (deepin-clone / dde partition tooling). The header banner reads 'corpus/train/train_0190_cwe61.cpp' and the sample tag 'primevul_train_2256_vuln', but the code was reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: Two trust boundaries. (1) partDevice, a caller-supplied QString device path, is interpolated directly into shell-tool command strings (lines 11 and 49) with no validation or quoting. (2) '/tmp' is a world-writable, shared directory; the fixed path '/tmp/partclone.log' (line 49) is predictable and pre-creatable by any unprivileged local user. A partition helper of this kind normally runs with elevated privilege (root, via a D-Bus/polkit service), so any local user who can trigger this code, influence partDevice, or plant a file in /tmp is the attacker. The scraped output of df/partclone is also semi-untrusted, but the primary hostile inputs are partDevice and the /tmp path.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes a partition device path (partDevice) and three optional output pointers (used, free, blockSize) that receive byte counts and the filesystem block size. It returns a bool success flag. It constructs a QProcess and copies the current environment, then forces 'LANG=C' so that the external tools emit parseable, locale-independent (English, dot-decimal) output. Forcing LANG=C is a correct hardening step for output scraping; it does not affect the security posture of the command strings themselves.

If the partition is currently mounted, the mounted-path branch runs 'df -B1 -P <partDevice>'. '-B1' asks df to report sizes in 1-byte units and '-P' selects POSIX single-line output. The %1 placeholder is filled with partDevice via QString::arg. It waits for df to finish, and if the exit code is non-zero it logs the captured stderr via dCError with a fixed '%s' format and returns false. Note the command is a single QString passed to QProcess::start, which performs its own whitespace/quote splitting into argv (no /bin/sh is spawned).

The df stdout is read, trimmed, and split on newlines. POSIX df prints a header line plus one data line, so exactly two lines are expected; anything else is rejected. The data line (lines.last()) is simplified() (collapses runs of whitespace to single spaces and trims) and split on single spaces into six fields: Filesystem, 1024-blocks/size, Used, Available, Capacity%, Mounted-on. A count other than 6 is rejected. These structural guards are defensive and reduce parser risk.

Field index 2 (Used bytes) and index 3 (Available bytes) are converted to qint64 with toLongLong, which sets ok=false on a non-numeric field. On any conversion failure the function returns false. On success both out-parameters are written (guarded by the non-null pointer checks) and the function returns true. Because '-B1' was used, these values are already in bytes, so no multiply is needed on this branch.

For an unmounted partition, it selects a partclone variant with getPartcloneExecuter(DDevicePartInfo(partDevice)) (e.g. partclone.ext4) and runs it as: '<executer> -s <partDevice> -c -q -C -L /tmp/partclone.log'. '-s' is the source device, '-c' clone mode, '-q' quiet, '-C' skip size check, '-L' names the log file. stdout is discarded to /dev/null and the read channel is set to stderr, because partclone prints its progress/size information to stderr. It then waits for the process to start. The log path '/tmp/partclone.log' is a hard-coded constant in a shared temp directory.

used_block and free_block are initialised to -1 sentinels. The loop reads partclone stderr in chunks (5s timeout per read), splits each chunk on newlines, and simplifies each line. Any line matching the word-boundary regex '\berror\b' is logged via dCError, but note it only logs — it does not abort — so an 'error' line does not stop parsing. The subsequent branches look for the specific stat lines partclone emits.

When a line begins 'Space in use:' the 7th token (index 6) is parsed as the used block count; when a line begins 'Free Space:' the 6th token (index 5) is parsed as the free block count. QByteArrayList::value(index, default) returns '-1' if the token is missing, so a malformed line yields -1 rather than an out-of-range access — a safe default that is later rejected by the negativity check. A non-numeric token sets ok=false and aborts. These values are block counts, not bytes, so a later multiply by block size is required.

The 'Block size:' line yields the third token (index 2) parsed as an int. This branch is treated as the terminal record: it verifies that used_block, free_block, and block_size are all non-negative (catching the -1 sentinels/defaults), then computes the byte counts as used_block * block_size and free_block * block_size and writes the out-parameters. It writes blockSize, terminates partclone, waits for it, and returns true. The ordering assumption is that 'Block size:' arrives after the two space lines have populated used_block/free_block; if it arrives first the sentinel check bails out cleanly.

If the read loop ends (timeout/EOF) without ever hitting the 'Block size:' terminal record, or if isMounted was false and partclone produced no usable output, the function falls through to return false. This is the default failure path.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The partclone log path is the fixed, predictable, world-known string '/tmp/partclone.log' in a shared, world-writable directory, and partclone is asked to open/write it via '-L'. Because this helper runs privileged (root, typical for a partition service), an unprivileged local attacker can pre-create '/tmp/partclone.log' as a symbolic link pointing at any file the victim process can write (e.g. /etc/passwd, a cron file, a config in another user's home). partclone then follows the link and writes/truncates the target with root privileges — the classic UNIX symlink-following attack (arbitrary file overwrite / corruption, potential privilege escalation). Even absent an attacker, a fixed shared path invites cross-user clobbering and info leak, since the log may contain device details and is left readable in /tmp. The safe design is a per-invocation, O_EXCL/mkstemp file created in a private, mode-0700 directory (or O_NOFOLLOW), never a static /tmp name.

partDevice is interpolated into a command STRING and handed to QProcess::start(const QString&), which does not spawn a shell but DOES split the string on whitespace (respecting quotes) into argv. An attacker who controls partDevice can therefore inject additional command-line arguments to df (and, on line 49, to the selected partclone binary). This is argument/option injection: a partDevice value containing spaces becomes extra argv entries, letting the attacker smuggle flags or extra operands into a privileged tool (partclone in particular takes many powerful flags such as an output/restore target). The invariant is that partDevice must be validated as a real block-device path and passed as a single explicit argument via the QStringList overload QProcess::start(program, arguments) rather than being formatted into a command string. Note this is not /bin/sh injection (no shell is invoked), so metacharacters like ';' are literal, but option injection into a root-run tool is still serious.

used_block/free_block are qint64 block counts parsed from partclone stderr and block_size is an int. The product used_block * block_size is evaluated in 64-bit signed arithmetic, but there is no upper bound on either factor beyond '>= 0' (line 101). A very large block count times a large block size can overflow qint64 and produce a negative or wrapped byte count in the out-parameter, which downstream callers may treat as a valid capacity (miscomputed free space, bad allocation sizing, or logic errors). The values come from a semi-trusted tool, so severity is limited, but the multiply lacks any overflow guard (CWE-190). The safe form checks that block_size and the counts are within sane ranges before multiplying.

There is a time-of-check/time-of-use gap between the isMounted(partDevice) test and the subsequent df/partclone invocation: the mount state (and the device the path resolves to) can change between the two, so the wrong branch/tool may run against a device that was swapped underneath. Combined with the lack of validation that partDevice denotes a genuine block device, this is a weaker, secondary robustness/validation concern (CWE-367/CWE-20) rather than the primary issue, but it compounds the argument-injection exposure above.

summary: The dominant issue is a predictable temp-file/symlink-following hazard: a privileged run of partclone is directed at the fixed path '/tmp/partclone.log' (line 49), letting a local user plant a symlink and cause a root-privileged arbitrary file overwrite. Secondary issues are argument/option injection because partDevice is formatted into command STRINGS passed to QProcess::start (lines 11, 49) instead of an explicit argument vector, plus an unbounded qint64 multiply (lines 105/108). The output-scraping guards themselves are reasonably defensive.

reviewed_anchors.reason: partDevice is an untrusted, unvalidated device path that flows into command strings; out-pointers receive computed sizes.
partDevice formatted into a QProcess command string -> argument/option injection into df.
Fixed format string with a single %s over a null-terminated QByteArray; not a format-string or NULL-deref bug (process object is a stack local, always valid).
Defensive structural check; rejects malformed df output before parsing.
Defensive field-count check bounding the subsequent at(2)/at(3) accesses.
Indices are guarded by the count==6 check and conversion failure is handled; values already in bytes (-B1), no multiply.
Fixed predictable /tmp log path (symlink following) AND partDevice/executer formatted into command string (argument injection).
Predictable path in world-writable shared directory used by a privileged process -> symlink-following arbitrary file overwrite.
Writing to /dev/null is safe; not a temp-file or injection surface.
Log-only; constdata is null-terminated; fixed format string; no control-flow or memory impact.
value(index,default) returns a safe default on missing token (no OOB); default -1 later rejected by negativity check.
Correctly rejects sentinel/default -1 values before use; catches negatives but not overflow.
Unbounded signed 64-bit multiply with no overflow guard (values are semi-trusted tool output).
TOCTOU / missing device-path validation compounding the injection surface (secondary).

findings.title: Symlink-following via predictable /tmp/partclone.log in a privileged process
findings.bug_class: Insecure temporary file / UNIX symlink following (arbitrary file overwrite)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0190_cwe61.cpp (path choice here); write performed by the partclone implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: A privileged process must never write to a fixed, attacker-predictable path in a shared world-writable directory; the log must be created in a private per-process directory or opened with O_EXCL/O_NOFOLLOW (mkstemp), so a pre-existing symlink cannot redirect the write.
findings.failure_mode: This helper runs with elevated privilege (partition management). An unprivileged local user pre-creates '/tmp/partclone.log' as a symlink to a sensitive file (e.g. /etc/passwd, a cron entry, another user's file). When partclone opens '-L /tmp/partclone.log', it follows the symlink and writes/truncates the target as root, corrupting or overwriting it and potentially escalating privileges. Even without an attacker, the fixed name allows cross-user denial-of-service and leaks device info left readable in /tmp.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the process runs privileged and that partclone opens '-L' without O_EXCL/O_NOFOLLOW; verify no prior code creates the file safely. Fix by switching to a mkstemp path under a mode-0700 per-process directory.

findings.title: Argument/option injection: partDevice formatted into QProcess command strings
findings.bug_class: Argument injection into external command (unvalidated input in command string)
findings.invariant: Untrusted operands must be passed as explicit, separate argv entries via QProcess::start(program, QStringList args) and validated to match a real block-device path; they must never be interpolated into a whitespace-split command string.
findings.failure_mode: QProcess::start(const QString&) splits the string on whitespace (honoring quotes) into argv without a shell. A partDevice containing spaces injects additional arguments into df or, more dangerously, into the root-run partclone binary (which accepts powerful flags for source/restore targets and behavior), enabling option smuggling. Because no /bin/sh is spawned, shell metacharacters are literal, so this is argument injection rather than shell command injection, but against a privileged tool it can still alter what the tool does.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether partDevice can be attacker-influenced and whether callers validate it; verify migration to the QStringList argument overload and a strict device-path allowlist (e.g. matches /dev/…).

findings.title: Unbounded signed 64-bit multiply of block count by block size
findings.bug_class: Signed integer overflow in size computation
findings.invariant: Before multiplying, the block count and block size must be range-checked so used_block * block_size and free_block * block_size cannot exceed qint64 range; otherwise the out-parameter must be treated as invalid.
findings.failure_mode: used_block/free_block (qint64) and block_size (int) are only checked to be non-negative (line 101). A pathologically large block count reported by partclone times a large block size overflows qint64 and yields a wrapped/negative byte total in *used or *free, which callers may consume as a real capacity, producing incorrect free-space or allocation decisions. Inputs are semi-trusted tool output, limiting severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify realistic bounds on partclone's reported block counts and block size, and add an overflow-safe multiply (e.g. __builtin_mul_overflow or a divide-based check) before writing the out-parameters.

audit_checklist: Confirm the privilege level this helper executes under (root/D-Bus service); the severity of both the /tmp symlink and the argument injection hinges on it.
Replace '/tmp/partclone.log' with a per-invocation file created via mkstemp in a mode-0700 private directory, or open with O_EXCL|O_NOFOLLOW; never a fixed shared-temp name.
Switch both process.start(QString) calls to QProcess::start(program, QStringList args) so partDevice and the partclone path are explicit, separate argv entries.
Validate partDevice against a strict block-device allowlist (e.g. canonical /dev/… path, no spaces/metacharacters) before use.
Add an overflow-safe multiply for used_block*block_size and free_block*block_size, and bound the parsed counts.
Verify the isMounted(partDevice)->start sequence has no exploitable TOCTOU (device swapped between check and use).
Confirm dCError's format arguments are all fixed format strings (they are) and that logged QByteArray values are properly terminated.
